article_items.title: Lloyds Banking Group to hire 300 tech experts to work on AI
article_items.description: Exclusive: While recruits will increase headcount for now, broader adoption of AI could lead to jobs cuts in future
article_items.excerpt: Exclusive: While recruits will increase headcount for now, broader adoption of AI could lead to jobs cuts in future
article_items.summary: The recruits – who will be part of a 1,000-strong AI team also made up of retrained Lloyds staff – will be deploying existing large language models such as Anthropic’s Claude and building on top of public LLMs such as Google’s Gemini to the bank’s own specifications. Davies said the AI cohort would be deployed to a range of projects, including identifying and preventing scams and fraud. It will change roles and how we work, and we are investing in training for colleagues through that transition.” In January, Nunn acknowledged that the bank would have to “ reduce some jobs in some areas” owing to AI. The group expects a £100m benefit this year, thanks to its growing use of agentic AI models. The bank said it intended the recruits to work on its use and development of agentic AI by September, referring to autonomous artificial intelligence models that can plan and execute tasks with minimal human oversight.

However, research suggests that some UK banks are becoming reliant on AI faster than they are preparing for outages of artificial intelligence.
Last month, Standard Chartered announced 7,000 job cuts, due in part to AI.

It will change roles and how we work, and we are investing in training for colleagues through that transition.” In January, Nunn acknowledged that the bank would have to “ reduce some jobs in some areas” owing to AI.
Some would be working on how AI models could be used internally, including to distill and search reams of documents in the HR department.
The group expects a £100m benefit this year, thanks to its growing use of agentic AI models.
Trystan Davies, group head of data and AI science, said: “AI will reshape how organisations are structured.
While the hiring drive is will increase Lloyds’ headcount for now, the group did not rule out its broad adoption of AI leading to job cuts in the future.

But one of the key focuses will be on making online banking more accessible and personalised, letting customers interrogate their spending habits, and ask plain language questions about their finances, including which.

article_items.title: What is the Nex Playground console, how much is it and when is it out in the UK?

New video game console aims to get kids moving The company behind the UK's newest video game console is not concerned with the latest state-of-the-art graphics or hardware. The parents I spoke to said their children often played on the console for between half an hour to an hour in one session, with the games typically being used as a way to transition into another activity, or to allow some structured play. Is it a myth – or a preview of the internet's future? Ahead of the UK launch I spoke to parents who already own the console in the US, and tried the machine myself to find out how it works.

Claude Guillemot, one of Ubisoft’s co-founders, has died in a plane crash The crash reportedly happened on an airstrip near the western coast of France on June 19.
Claude Guillemot, one of five brothers who co-founded Ubisoft in 1986, has died, the company confirmed.
As first reported by Ouest France , Guillemot, 69, was one of two victims involved in a Cessna 421 plane crash in the afternoon of June 19.
"Ubisoft was deeply saddened to learn of the death of Claude Guillemot, co-founder of the group and chairman of Guillemot Corp., in an accident," Ubisoft said in a statement.
According to the report, local firefighters said the crash happened in a field near La Baule airport, on the western coast of France.
Emergency responders said that the aircraft was on fire when they arrived and was spreading to the nearby environment.
Guillemot was also the chairman and CEO of Guillemot Corp., which owns DJ equipment maker Hercules and gaming peripheral company Thrustmaster.
Guillemot also served on Ubisoft's board of directors, while his brother, Yves, is still the director, chairman and CEO of Ubisoft.

No further statements will be made at this time." Along with his brothers, Guillemot grew Ubisoft into a major video game publisher headquartered in France that has successful franchises, like Assassin's Creed and Far.
"Our thoughts are with his family and loved ones during this difficult time.
